sections.header: Verses 1-14 — From Abandoned Infant to Adorned Queen

heb.paragraph: Jerusalem's origins are Canaanite: 'Your father was an Amorite and your mother a Hittite.' This is not genealogy but theology — Jerusalem has no innate claim to holiness. Before David conquered it, the city was Jebusite. God chose Jerusalem not because of her pedigree but despite her pagan origins.

panels.ctx: Chapter 16 is the longest chapter in Ezekiel and one of the most shocking allegories in Scripture. God recounts Jerusalem's history as a story of a foundling child: born of pagan parents, abandoned at birth with umbilical cord uncut, left in a field to die. God passed by, commanded the infant to live, then returned when she reached womanhood, covered her nakedness, entered a covenant of marriage, and adorned her as a queen. The allegory covers the entire span from pre-Israelite Jerusalem through covenant, kingdom, and apostasy.

cross.note: God chose Israel not because of size or merit but out of love — the same grace that rescued the foundling.
Hosea's marriage allegory parallels Ezekiel's but from the husband's perspective rather than the wife's history.
Christ loved the church and gave himself for her to present her radiant — the NT marriage typology echoes the foundling-to-bride pattern.

notes.note: Father an Amorite, mother a Hittite — Jerusalem's pre-Israelite identity. The city had no inherent holiness; its sanctity derived entirely from God's choice.
The double command 'Live!' spoken to the blood-covered infant is the word of sovereign grace — creating life where there was only death.
The spreading of the garment corner signifies covenant marriage (cf. Ruth 3:9). God entered a binding relationship with Jerusalem through the Sinai covenant.
Jerusalem's beauty became perfect through the splendor God bestowed. Her renown spread among nations — but the beauty was entirely a gift, not an inherent quality.

Calvin uses the abandoned infant as a picture of total depravity: humanity left to itself has no spiritual life, no beauty, no hope. Only the sovereign 'Live!' of God creates spiritual vitality from death.
Calvin stresses that every adornment came from God: Jerusalem's beauty, wealth, and fame were gifts, not earnings. This makes the subsequent betrayal all the more heinous.

The practices described (cutting the umbilical cord, washing, rubbing with salt, wrapping in cloths) reflect actual ancient Near Eastern birth customs. The absence of these rites signals total abandonment.
The phrase 'I spread the corner of my garment over you' (eknaf) is a marriage formula attested in Ruth 3:9 and in ancient Near Eastern marriage contracts.

Block reads the allegory as a covenant history in narrative form: birth (pre-election), first passing (the Abrahamic promise), growth (the patriarchal period), second passing with marriage (Sinai covenant), and royal glory (Davidic-Solomonic era). Each phase corresponds to a stage in Israel's actual history.

Zimmerli identifies the genre as a juridical parable (Rechtsparabel) — a story that draws the audience in before revealing that they are the guilty party. The narrative beauty of vv.1-14 makes the betrayal of vv.15ff all the more devastating when the identification is made.

heb.gloss: to commit harlotry / to be unfaithful
heb.paragraph: The verb zanah (and its derivatives) occurs over 20 times in this chapter alone. Ezekiel uses sexual betrayal as the governing metaphor for idolatry because it captures the personal, covenantal, and emotional dimensions of Israel's unfaithfulness. Idolatry is not merely a ritual mistake but an intimate betrayal.

Jerusalem's prostitution is described as worse than ordinary harlotry — she pays her lovers instead of being paid (v.33-34). This inverts even the economics of prostitution: Israel gave away God's gifts to attract foreign alliances and foreign gods, receiving nothing in return.

panels.ctx: The allegory details Jerusalem's unfaithfulness in three directions: religious apostasy (using God's gifts for idol worship, vv.15-22), political alliances (Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, vv.23-29), and child sacrifice (vv.20-21). The most horrifying detail: she sacrificed her own children — the children God gave her — to the idols. The language is deliberately graphic to provoke shock and revulsion, forcing the audience to see their history through God's eyes.

cross.note: Hosea's marriage to Gomer dramatizes the same theme: God as betrayed husband, Israel as unfaithful wife.
Ahaz sacrificed his son in the fire according to the detestable practices of the nations — the historical reality behind Ezekiel's allegory.
Jeremiah condemned the child sacrifice at Topheth in the Valley of Ben Hinnom — the same abomination.

notes.note: Jerusalem used the beauty and wealth God gave her to attract foreign gods and foreign powers. Every gift was diverted from its intended purpose.
Child sacrifice — 'You slaughtered my children and sacrificed them to the idols.' The possessive 'my children' intensifies the crime: they belonged to God.
At every street corner she built high places and degraded her beauty — the public and shameless nature of the apostasy.
The ultimate inversion: a prostitute who pays her clients instead of receiving payment. Israel's apostasy defies even the logic of sin.

Calvin reads this as a warning that the most dangerous temptation follows the greatest blessing. Jerusalem's beauty became the occasion of her fall precisely because she forgot the Giver and worshiped the gifts.
The 'colorful shrines' (bamot) decorated with garments God had given her represent the transformation of covenant gifts into instruments of idolatry.
Egypt as a lover 'great of flesh' is a crude anatomical reference emphasizing the degrading nature of the political alliance.

Block organizes the lovers geographically: Egypt (south), Philistia (west), Assyria (northeast), Babylon (east). Jerusalem prostituted herself in every direction — her unfaithfulness was comprehensive, not selective. The compass of betrayal covers the entire known world.

Zimmerli sees child sacrifice as the theological nadir of the allegory: the foundling child who was saved from death now sacrifices her own children to death. The rescued becomes the destroyer — the most devastating reversal in the entire book.

sections.header: Verses 35-63 — Judgment, Shame, and the Everlasting Covenant

heb.paragraph: Despite the graphic judgment, the chapter ends with God establishing an 'everlasting covenant' (berit olam) with Jerusalem. This is not a reward for repentance but an act of sovereign grace: God remembers his covenant even when Jerusalem has forgotten. The atonement comes from God, not from Jerusalem's initiative. Grace has the last word.

panels.ctx: The judgment section (vv.35-43) employs covenant lawsuit language: God gathers Jerusalem's lovers to strip her naked and execute the sentence for adultery and bloodshed. But the chapter does not end with judgment. In a stunning reversal, God compares Jerusalem unfavorably to her 'sisters' Samaria and Sodom (vv.44-52), then promises restoration through an everlasting covenant (vv.59-63). The final note is not wrath but grace — and the response is not pride but shame. Jerusalem will be silenced by forgiveness.

cross.note: The new covenant promise parallels Ezekiel's everlasting covenant — both emerge from judgment, both are grounded in divine initiative.
Where sin increased, grace increased all the more — Paul's principle matches the movement of this chapter from maximum sin to maximum grace.
The prodigal son parable follows the same pattern: a child who squanders the father's gifts, experiences degradation, and is restored by grace that produces shame rather than pride.

notes.note: God gathers all her lovers against her — the very nations she courted will become the instruments of judgment. Political alliances become military threats.
Jerusalem is worse than Sodom. The comparison is devastating: the byword for wickedness is more righteous than God's own city.
Sodom's sin was arrogance, abundance of food, and careless ease while ignoring the poor and needy. Social injustice, not just sexual sin, defines Sodom's wickedness.
Despite everything, God will establish an everlasting covenant. The restoration produces not self-congratulation but shame — genuine repentance that is silenced by the magnitude of grace.

Calvin marvels at the everlasting covenant: after rehearsing the most horrifying catalog of sin in the OT, God ends with unconditional grace. The covenant stands because God remembers, not because Jerusalem repents.
Sodom's sin explicitly includes neglecting the poor — a corrective to readings that reduce Sodom's wickedness to a single category of sin.
Receiving Samaria and Sodom as 'daughters' means Jerusalem's restoration will include and surpass all others. The scope of the new covenant exceeds the old.

Block sees the everlasting covenant as Ezekiel's gospel: grace follows judgment as certainly as judgment follows sin. The shame that silences Jerusalem is not humiliation but the proper response to unmerited forgiveness — the recognition that one has been loved beyond all deserving.

Zimmerli identifies the covenant renewal as unilateral: God establishes (heqim) rather than cuts (karat) the covenant, using language of reaffirmation rather than new initiation. God honors his original commitment despite total human failure.

rows.label: vv.6-14
rows.text: Rescue, Covenant Marriage, and Royal Glory

lit.note: The longest chapter in Ezekiel: Jerusalem's entire history as a love story turned tragedy turned grace. From foundling to queen to harlot to recipient of an everlasting covenant.

themes.note: Covenant peaks at 10: the entire chapter is structured around covenant — established, violated, and renewed. Judgment at 9 but Mercy at 7: the everlasting covenant restores what judgment destroyed.

hebtext.note: Jerusalem's origins are Canaanite: 'Your father was an Amorite and your mother a Hittite.' This is not genealogy but theology — Jerusalem has no innate claim to holiness. Before David conquered it, the city was Jebusite. God chose Jerusalem not because of her pedigree but despite her pagan origins.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Chapter 16 is the longest chapter in Ezekiel and one of the most shocking allegories in Scripture…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Unfaithful Wife

translations.text: I made you grow like a plant of the field. You grew and developed and entered puberty. Your breasts had formed and your hair had grown, yet you were stark naked.
I made you flourish like a plant of the field. And you grew up and became tall and arrived at full adornment. Your breasts were formed, and your hair had grown; yet you were naked and bare.
On the day you were born your cord was not cut, nor were you washed with water to make you clean, nor were you rubbed with salt or wrapped in cloths.
And as for your birth, on the day you were born your cord was not cut, nor were you washed with water to cleanse you, nor rubbed with salt, nor wrapped in swaddling cloths.
